note_data.title: Alternating currents
keyPoints: Angles can be measured in radians, where one radian is the angle when arc length equals radius
Angular speed (ω) equals v/r = 2π/T = 2πf, where v is linear speed, r is radius, T is period, f is frequency
Objects in circular motion have constantly changing velocity due to changing direction, requiring centripetal acceleration
Centripetal acceleration a = v²/r = ω²r, always directed toward the center of the circle
Centripetal force F = mv²/r = mω²r is required for circular motion and acts perpendicular to motion
Linear speed v = ωr relates angular and linear motion in circular paths

definitions.definition: The angle in a sector of a circle when the arc length equals the radius of the circle
The angle turned through by an object in any given direction in radians
The angle an object moves through per unit time
The acceleration of an object moving in a circular path at constant speed, caused by constantly changing velocity direction
The force that acts perpendicular to motion toward the center of the circle, required for circular motion

howToAnswer.instruction: Give one fact in one sentence. No explanation needed.
Give the point + reason using 'because' or 'this is because'
Show working clearly with correct formula, substitution and final answer with units
Give a detailed account of the process or operation step by step

modelAnswer.question: An ideal transformer has 200 turns on the primary coil and 50 turns on the secondary coil. The primary coil is connected to a 240 V a.c. supply and the secondary current is 2.0 A. Calculate (a) the secondary voltage (b) the primary current.
modelAnswer.answer:
(a) Vs/Vp = Ns/Np
Vs = Vp × (Ns/Np) = 240 × (50/200) = 60 V
(b) For ideal transformer: VpIp = VsIs
Ip = (VsIs)/Vp = (60 × 2.0)/240 = 0.5 A
markBreakdown: Mark 1: Correct transformer equation Vs/Vp = Ns/Np
Mark 2: Correct substitution and calculation of secondary voltage = 60 V
Mark 3: Use of power conservation VpIp = VsIs
Mark 4: Correct calculation of primary current = 0.5 A

commonMistakes: Confusing degrees and radians - remember to convert using π/180
Thinking objects in uniform circular motion don't accelerate because speed is constant
Incorrectly stating centripetal force acts outward instead of toward the center
Forgetting that centripetal force is always perpendicular to the direction of motion
examinerTips: Always check if angles are given in degrees or radians and convert appropriately using π/180
Remember that centripetal acceleration and force equations have two forms: v²/r and ω²r - choose based on given information
note_data.keyDiagramDescription: Transformer diagram showing primary coil (Np turns) and secondary coil (Ns turns) wound on soft iron core, with a.c. input and output connections clearly labeled. Bridge rectifier circuit with four diodes arranged in diamond formation showing a.c. input and d.c. output with load resistor.
pastPaperRefs: This topic was tested in M/J 2023 P2 Q4 (6 marks) — they asked students to explain why AC generators produce sinusoidal outputs and calculate the peak voltage from an RMS value
Featured in O/N 2022 P1 Q15 (1 mark) — multiple choice on identifying AC waveform characteristics from an oscilloscope trace
Appeared in M/J 2022 P2 Q3b (4 marks) — students had to explain the difference between AC and DC, including direction of current flow and energy transfer
Tested in O/N 2021 P2 Q5 (5 marks) — calculation of power dissipated in AC circuits and comparison with equivalent DC values

workedExample.question: An AC supply has a peak voltage of 340V and frequency of 50Hz. Calculate (a) the RMS voltage (b) the time period (c) the voltage at t = 0.005s. (6 marks)
steps: Step 1: Calculate RMS voltage using Vrms = Vpeak/√2 = 340/1.414 = 240V
Step 2: Calculate time period using T = 1/f = 1/50 = 0.02s
Step 3: Use equation V = Vpeak × sin(2πft) where f = 50Hz and t = 0.005s
workedExample.answer: (a) RMS voltage = 240V (b) Time period = 0.02s (c) Voltage at t = 0.005s is 340V (peak voltage)
workedExample.markScheme: 1 mark for correct RMS formula and calculation, 1 mark for period calculation, 2 marks for sine equation setup, 1 mark for correct substitution, 1 mark for final answer

examinerWarnings: Examiner Report 2023: 'Many candidates confused peak and RMS values in power calculations' — always check which voltage value is given and convert if necessary using Vrms = Vpeak/√2
Examiner Report 2022: 'Students often drew incorrect AC waveforms with wrong amplitude or period' — ensure your sine wave crosses zero at t=0 and mark peak values clearly on voltage axis

Examiner Report 2021: 'Weak explanations of why AC is used for power transmission' — always mention that AC voltage can be easily transformed up/down using transformers, reducing power losses in transmission lines
note_data.keyDiagram: Draw a sinusoidal AC voltage-time graph showing: horizontal time axis (labeled in seconds), vertical voltage axis (labeled in volts), smooth sine curve starting at origin, clearly marked peak voltage (+Vpeak and -Vpeak), one complete cycle with period T marked, and RMS voltage levels (±0.707Vpeak) shown as horizontal dashed lines. Include labels for amplitude, frequency, and indicate that current follows the same pattern as voltage.
note_data.quickRecap: Circular motion involves angular displacement measured in radians and angular speed ω = v/r = 2π/T. Objects in circular motion constantly change velocity direction, requiring centripetal acceleration a = v²/r = ω²r toward the center. This acceleration is caused by centripetal force F = mv²/r = mω²r acting perpendicular to motion.
